prompt:
On Saturday morning I unlocked the back door of the town print shop expecting three quiet hours of dusting. The presses had been idle long enough for the ink to smell faintly sweet instead of sharp, and the trays of metal type looked, at first, like relics that no one sensible would still bother to sort. Mr. Bell, who had set headlines there for nearly forty years, handed me a soft brush and began lifting letters from one drawer to another. Each time he paused over a bent piece of type or a handle polished smooth by use, he attached a story to it: the school-election bulletin that had to be printed before dawn, the harvest poster corrected at the last minute when a farmer noticed a misspelled name, the winter afternoon when the furnace quit and everyone worked in coats until the final stack was tied.
By lunchtime, I had cleaned only a few drawers. Yet the smudges on the cases no longer looked like simple dirt. They looked like evidence that the room had been depended on, repaired, and depended on again. Near the doorway hung a framed photograph of the shop taken years earlier. In it, the tables were spotless, the windows bright, and no one stood at the press. On my way out, I stopped in front of the picture longer than I expected. For the first time, the image seemed incomplete rather than accurate.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The narrator discovers that printing is much more difficult than expected.
choices.B: Mr. Bell convinces the narrator to work at the print shop permanently.
choices.C: Hearing Mr. Bell’s stories changes the narrator’s understanding of the old print shop and its tools.
choices.D: The narrator decides that the shop should become a museum instead of remaining open.

explanation: The narrator begins with a routine, skeptical attitude and ends by seeing the shop differently because of the stories attached to its objects.

choices.A: owned the print shop for nearly forty years
choices.B: set headlines in the print shop for nearly forty years
choices.C: taken the photograph hanging near the doorway
choices.D: taught the narrator how to operate every press in the shop

explanation: The passage states this directly when introducing Mr. Bell.

choices.D: carefully organized and ready for use

prompt:
Many libraries have long arranged study space under a simple assumption: people work best when distractions are minimized and each person faces outward in a separate carrel. A recent study of neighborhood libraries suggests that this design serves some users well but does not capture all of the ways people study. In several branches, researchers added a small zone near the entrance with round tables, shared outlets, and a whiteboard listing common assignment deadlines for local schools. In similar branches nearby, the seating remained mostly rows of individual desks and carrels.
The researchers found that students in the mixed-layout branches were more likely to stay after finishing an immediate task. Some reviewed notes with a classmate, asked a librarian a follow-up question, or used the whiteboard to compare due dates. These students did not report being less focused overall. Instead, many said that having both private desks and a small shared area made it easier to shift between concentrated work and quick collaboration. In the branches with only individual seating, students often completed their original task and left promptly.
The study does not argue that every library should replace quiet carrels with group tables. Silence and privacy remain essential for many readers and test-takers. Rather, the researchers suggest that a modest range of spaces may support a wider range of study habits than a single layout can.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Students concentrate best when every library uses only individual carrels.
choices.B: A mix of private and shared study spaces may support more kinds of study behavior than a single layout does.
choices.C: Whiteboards are the main reason students finish their work on time.
choices.D: Library users dislike quiet spaces when round tables are available.

explanation: The passage argues that having both quiet and collaborative spaces better serves varied study habits.

prompt:
Some readers skim stage directions as if they were instructions meant only for actors and directors. When the directions are dull, that impulse is understandable. A note that merely tells us a character crosses the room or sits in a chair may seem less vivid than the dialogue surrounding it. Yet in many plays, the best stage directions do more than manage movement. They establish rhythm, clarify tension, and quietly shape the reader’s sense of what kind of world the play inhabits. A direction that a door “refuses to close cleanly,” for example, may matter less for mechanics than for mood.
This matters because plays are built from what is spoken and what is withheld. Dialogue can suggest conflict, but stage directions often control its pace and pressure. They tell us whether a pause feels embarrassed, threatening, or exhausted. They can also keep a scene from becoming too abstract by anchoring it in sound, gesture, and space. In that sense, stage directions are not decorations added after the fact; they are part of the play’s meaning.
I once watched students read a short scene twice, first without the directions and then with them restored. In the stripped version, the exchange sounded merely sharp. With the directions returned, one character’s habit of straightening crooked objects during an argument made the same exchange feel tense and defensive. The words had not changed, but the scene had acquired a more specific temperature.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Stage directions are mainly useful for tracking actors’ movements onstage.
choices.B: Good stage directions shape meaning and tone rather than serving as mere technical instructions.
choices.C: Dialogue is more important than stage directions in every kind of play.
choices.D: Modern plays contain too many stage directions for readers to follow.

explanation: The passage argues that stage directions do more than manage movement; they help determine mood, rhythm, and meaning.

explanation: The first paragraph says a plain direction can seem less vivid than the dialogue surrounding it.

prompt:
Some readers skim stage directions as if they were instructions meant only for actors and directors. When the directions are dull, that impulse is understandable. A note that merely tells us a character crosses the room or sits in a chair may seem less vivid than the dialogue surrounding it. Yet in many plays, the best stage directions do more than manage movement. They establish rhythm, clarify tension, and quietly shape the reader’s sense of what kind of world the play inhabits. A direction that a door “refuses to close cleanly,” for example, may matter less for mechanics than for mood.
This matters because plays are built from what is spoken and what is withheld. Dialogue can suggest conflict, but stage directions often control its pace and pressure. They tell us whether a pause feels embarrassed, threatening, or exhausted. They can also keep a scene from becoming too abstract by anchoring it in sound, gesture, and space. In that sense, stage directions are not decorations added after the fact; they are part of the play’s meaning.
I once watched students read a short scene twice, first without the directions and then with them restored. In the stripped version, the exchange sounded merely sharp. With the directions returned, one character’s habit of straightening crooked objects during an argument made the same exchange feel tense and defensive. The words had not changed, but the scene had acquired a more specific temperature.
Question:
As used in the final sentence, “a more specific temperature” most nearly means:

choices.A: a clearer emotional atmosphere
choices.B: a more accurate season of the year
choices.C: a room that felt physically warmer
choices.D: a stage with brighter lighting

explanation: “A more specific temperature” refers figuratively to the scene’s more precise emotional quality, not literal heat.

choices.A: argue that dialogue should be removed from most plays

choices.C: show that stage directions are often longer than dialogue
choices.D: compare comedy with tragedy in technical terms

explanation: The second paragraph explains how directions shape pauses, mood, and the concrete feel of a scene.

prompt:
For many years, beach managers removed piles of seaweed, shells, and drift material—known collectively as wrack—from the upper shore as part of routine cleanup. The reasoning seemed obvious: cleaner sand looked tidier to visitors. More recent field studies suggest that wrack does more than clutter the beach. Because wrack traps moisture and shade near the sand surface, it can create cooler conditions for insects and other small invertebrates. It can also slow wind at ground level, helping loose sand accumulate around young dune plants.
To test how wrack affects beach ecology, researchers compared stretches where wrack had been left in place with nearby stretches where it had been removed regularly. The beaches with wrack contained more ghost crab burrows and more seedlings of dune grasses by late summer. Sand temperatures near the surface also rose less sharply there during the hottest part of the day.
The researchers do not argue that wrack should be left everywhere without exception. Near heavy swimming areas or places where public access must remain clear, removal may still be necessary. Even so, the study suggests that treating all wrack as mere trash can overlook the role it plays in supporting habitat and stabilizing sand.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Wrack should always be removed from beaches to improve public access.
choices.B: Wrack can support habitat and sand stability, so removing it automatically may oversimplify its role.
choices.C: Ghost crabs can live only on beaches without wrack.
choices.D: Beach ecology depends mainly on shell size rather than plant growth.

explanation: The passage argues that wrack is more than trash because it helps create habitat and stabilize sand.
